network.overall_strategy: The network converges on lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropanal) as the key carbonyl precursor for threonine, following the literature consensus (Ritson & Sutherland 2013; meteoritic Strecker analyses). Three main branches feed into threonine aminonitrile: (1) cyanosulfidic UV photoredox chain reducing glycolonitrile → glycolaldehyde/acetaldehyde → lactonitrile → lactaldehyde, followed by Strecker assembly; (2) volcanic spark-discharge generation of HCN, NH₃, HCHO, and acetaldehyde as direct precursors; (3) HCN oligomerization to aminomalononitrile (AMN) followed by condensation with acetaldehyde (Thanassi 1975). Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation on greigite/magnetite provides formate, formaldehyde, and FTT-derived acetaldehyde, creating cross-environmental flow. HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile serve as hub molecules where multiple pathways converge. Final hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine occurs under mild aqueous (Biochemical) conditions.

reactions.name: Volcanic spark discharge synthesis of reactive C-N intermediates

conditions.other: Reducing gas mixture including CH₄, NH₃, H₂S, CO₂; products rain out into tidal/surface waters; H₂S-rich variant per Parker et al. 2011

reactions.mechanism: High-energy electrical discharge in a reducing volcanic plume dissociates CH₄, N₂, and H₂O into radicals (CH₃·, NH·, OH·, CN·). Radical recombination produces HCN, NH₃, and small carbonyl species including formaldehyde and acetaldehyde. Products condense and accumulate in surface waters. Both HCN and aldehydes are necessary Strecker precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Parker et al. (2011, PNAS) reanalyzed Miller's 1958 H₂S-rich spark discharge residues and identified 23 amino acids including threonine, with racemic D/L ratios supporting abiotic origin. Ring et al. (1972, PNAS) confirmed threonine and allothreonine in CH₄/N₂/H₂O discharge products. Parker et al. explicitly argue volcanic lightning in reducing plumes generates HCN and aldehydes that rain out into tidal settings for subsequent aqueous-phase Strecker assembly.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, H₂-rich serpentinization fluid, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed onto the greigite (Fe₃S₄) surface, which is structurally analogous to biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters. H₂ from serpentinization provides reducing equivalents. Two-electron reduction of CO₂ yields formate (HCOO⁻) as the initial C₁ fixation product.
reactions.reasoning: CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite and mackinawite surfaces is well-documented in alkaline hydrothermal vent chemistry. This provides the initial C₁ building block from which further reduction to formaldehyde is possible. The formate produced here enters the surface environment where it feeds into the cyanosulfidic network as a C₁ source, bridging hydrothermal and surface chemistries.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduction of formate to formaldehyde

conditions.other: Subseafloor hydrothermal fluid, moderate pH, continued H₂ supply from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: Formate adsorbed on magnetite surface undergoes further two-electron reduction by H₂ to yield formaldehyde (HCHO). The magnetite surface stabilizes the transition state and provides a template for ordered reduction. HCHO is a critical C₁ building block for cyanohydrin and formose chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis on magnetite surfaces produces a range of oxygenated C₁-C₃ compounds from CO₂/CO + H₂. Formaldehyde is a key early intermediate in this progression. Andersson & Holm (2000, Orig. Life Evol. Biosph.) discuss pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite mineral buffers as controlling redox conditions for hydrothermal organic synthesis. The HCHO produced can be transported to surface environments via vent fluids.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Black smoker or subseafloor hydrothermal system; CO₂ + H₂ → CO intermediate → C₂ oxygenates

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is first reduced to CO on mineral surfaces, then CO undergoes Fischer-Tropsch chain growth: CO insertion and hydrogenation steps on the magnetite/iron surface yield C₂ oxygenates including acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO). Fe⁰ provides strong reducing power. This is a well-documented FTT product spectrum.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite produces alcohols, aldehydes, and hydrocarbons from CO/CO₂ + H₂ at 400-500K. Acetaldehyde is a key C₂ carbonyl product. This provides an independent hydrothermal source of acetaldehyde, complementing volcanic discharge (rxn_001) and cyanosulfidic photoredox (rxn_006) routes. Establishing multiple routes to this hub molecule is critical for network robustness.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: HCN + formaldehyde → glycolonitrile

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline aqueous pool; CN⁻ nucleophilic addition to HCHO carbonyl; near-quantitative at modest concentrations

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide ion (CN⁻, from HCN at alkaline pH) attacks the electrophilic carbon of formaldehyde in a nucleophilic addition to form the cyanohydrin glycolonitrile (HOCH₂CN). This is a rapid, thermodynamically favorable reaction at ambient temperature. Phosphate buffers maintain pH in the 7-9 range needed for sufficient CN⁻ concentration.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) use glycolonitrile as the starting substrate for their cyanosulfidic photoredox network, and note that it forms readily from HCN + HCHO. They explicitly use sodium phosphate buffer (33 mM) in their experimental system. Glycolonitrile serves as the entry point into the photoredox reduction cascade that ultimately yields lactaldehyde. HCN can be concentrated via ferrocyanide complexation with Fe²⁺ as noted by Ritson & Sutherland.

reactions.name: UV cyanosulfidic photoredox of glycolonitrile to glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation at 254 nm, 2 h; 10 mM glycolonitrile, 30 mM H₂S, 33 mM sodium phosphate, quartz cuvette; glycolaldehyde 42% yield; acetaldehyde as co-product via rearrangement

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of CuCN generates hydrated electrons (eₐq⁻) via Cu(I)/Cu(II) cyanocuprate photoredox cycling. H₂S serves as terminal reductant, regenerating Cu(I). Hydrated electrons reduce the nitrile group of glycolonitrile through a radical anion intermediate, leading to C-N bond cleavage and formation of glycolaldehyde (as hydrate). Acetaldehyde arises as a rearrangement/degradation co-product. CuCN forms a fine dark precipitate consistent with heterogeneous surface-mediated photocatalysis.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) report that UV irradiation (254 nm) of glycolonitrile with H₂S and CuCN yields glycolaldehyde in 42% yield after 2 h, alongside acetaldehyde and formaldehyde as side products. Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) elaborate the mechanism as Cu(I)/Cu(II) cyanocuprate photoredox cycling generating hydrated electrons. Ritson & Sutherland note that CuCN could be sourced geochemically from dissolution of copper sulfide minerals in cyanide solution.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: acetaldehyde + HCN → lactonitrile (acetaldehyde cyanohydrin)

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline aqueous pool; CN⁻ addition to acetaldehyde carbonyl; produces racemic lactonitrile (2-hydroxypropanenitrile)

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide ion (CN⁻) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde in nucleophilic addition, forming the cyanohydrin lactonitrile (CH₃CH(OH)CN). This is an equilibrium reaction favored at moderate cyanide concentrations and mildly alkaline pH. The product retains a hydroxyl group at C2, which is essential for the threonine sidechain.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) explicitly identify acetaldehyde cyanohydrin (lactonitrile) as a key intermediate in their 'second-stage' photoredox reductions. It is the direct substrate for the photoredox step that yields lactaldehyde—the dedicated threonine Strecker precursor. Cyanohydrin formation from aldehydes + HCN is a well-established, thermodynamically favorable reaction under mild aqueous conditions.

reactions.name: UV cyanosulfidic photoredox of lactonitrile to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation at 254 nm, 2 h; lactaldehyde yield 19%; quartz vessel; H₂S as terminal reductant

reactions.mechanism: UV-generated hydrated electrons from Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling reduce the nitrile group of lactonitrile (acetaldehyde cyanohydrin) through a radical anion intermediate, cleaving the C≡N bond and producing lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropanal, CH₃CH(OH)CHO). H₂S regenerates the Cu(I) state. This is the rate-limiting and yield-limiting step for threonine precursor formation in the cyanosulfidic network.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) explicitly report: 'irradiation of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin furnished lactaldehyde in 19% yield (after 2 h irradiation).' They identify lactaldehyde as 'the Strecker precursor of (allo-)threonine.' This 19% yield represents the key bottleneck in the photoredox route. Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) situate this step within the broader cyanosulfidic network and note constraints on CuCN availability (low crustal copper abundance restricts this to copper-rich terrains).

reactions.name: Surface Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: Mildly alkaline pool; ammonia-rich conditions favor aminonitrile over cyanohydrin pathway; timescale days to weeks; produces racemic mixture of threonine and allothreonine aminonitriles

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with NH₃ to form an imine (Schiff base, CH₃CH(OH)CH=NH). Nucleophilic addition of CN⁻ (from HCN) to the imine carbon yields the α-aminonitrile 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile—the threonine Strecker intermediate. The two stereocenters (C2, C3) yield diastereomeric products: threonine aminonitrile and allothreonine aminonitrile. Ammonia-rich conditions are essential; ammonia-poor conditions divert to cyanohydrin (→ hydroxy acid) pathway.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson & Sutherland (2013, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.) report that when HCN and NH₃ were added to photoreduction products, lactaldehyde was 'smoothly converted' via its cyanohydrin to the (allo-)threonine Strecker intermediate aminonitrile (compound 22 in their numbering). Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) embed this step in their systems-level network. The meteoritic Strecker-cyanohydrin analysis (Kyushu University report) emphasizes that ammonia availability controls whether amino acid vs. hydroxy acid products dominate, and explicitly identifies lactaldehyde as the carbonyl precursor for threonine/allothreonine.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: Redox buffered by FeS₂/FeS/Fe₃O₄ assemblage; pH buffered by K-feldspar-muscovite-quartz; caution: hydroxy amino acids susceptible to thermal degradation at high T; threonine specifically not yet confirmed in hydrothermal Strecker experiments

reactions.mechanism: Same Strecker mechanism as rxn_009 (imine formation + CN⁻ addition) but operating at elevated temperature and pressure in a mineral-buffered hydrothermal setting. The pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite assemblage controls oxygen fugacity (redox), while silicate buffers control pH. Higher temperature accelerates reaction kinetics but also promotes competing degradation (dehydration) of the hydroxy amino acid product.
reactions.reasoning: Andersson & Holm (2000, Orig. Life Evol. Biosph.) summarize hydrothermal Strecker experiments by Hennet et al. (1992) using HCN, CH₂O, and NH₄Cl at 150°C and 10 bar with pyrite-pyrrhotite-magnetite buffer, producing glycine (~9-12 mM) and alanine (~0.5-1.3 mM). However, threonine is NOT explicitly tabulated among products in those experiments—this route is therefore speculative for threonine specifically. The literature cautions that hydroxy amino acids undergo dehydration and degradation during hydrothermal exposure (USGS report on diagenetic amino acid reactions). This reaction requires lactaldehyde transport from surface to vent-flank environments.

reactions.name: HCN trimerization to aminomalononitrile (AMN)

conditions.other: Requires moderate HCN concentration (>0.01 M); clay surfaces adsorb and concentrate HCN; competes with HCN polymerization to intractable tholins; ferrocyanide pathway can pre-concentrate cyanide via Fe²⁺ complexation

reactions.mechanism: Three molecules of HCN undergo stepwise oligomerization: HCN dimerizes to iminoacetonitrile (or its tautomer), which then reacts with a third HCN to form aminomalononitrile (H₂N-CH(CN)₂). AMN is a key branch point in HCN chemistry: it can further react with a fourth HCN to yield diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN, adenine precursor), or it can serve as a multifunctional C-N nucleophile for condensation with electrophilic aldehydes.
reactions.reasoning: Thanassi (1975, J. Mol. Evol.) uses aminomalononitrile as a starting material for amino acid synthesis including threonine/allothreonine, establishing AMN as a legitimate prebiotic C-N building block. Green, Xu & Sutherland (2021, JACS) discuss the broader HCN oligomerization landscape, noting that AMN accumulation at useful concentrations remains an open question due to competing polymerization to intractable HCN polymers. Ritson & Sutherland (2013) note cyanide can be concentrated via ferrocyanide complexation.

reactions.name: AMN condensation with acetaldehyde → threonine/allothreonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: Mild aqueous conditions; AMN acts as carbanion-equivalent nucleophile; product is α-aminonitrile bearing hydroxyl sidechain; subsequent hydrolysis yields threonine/allothreonine

reactions.mechanism: Aminomalononitrile (H₂N-CH(CN)₂) acts as a stabilized carbanion equivalent: one nitrile group is displaced or hydrolyzed during condensation with acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO). The AMN nitrogen provides the amino group while acetaldehyde provides the C2-C3 fragment with the hydroxyl-bearing sidechain. The product is an α-aminonitrile equivalent (threonine aminonitrile) or a closely related intermediate that converges on the same hydrolysis product. The dinitrile character of AMN facilitates initial C-C bond formation via stabilized enolization. Both threonine and allothreonine diastereomers are produced.
reactions.reasoning: Thanassi (1975, J. Mol. Evol.) reports that reacting aminomalononitrile with electrophiles (including aldehydes) followed by hydrolysis yields a range of amino acids, explicitly identifying threonine and allothreonine in hydrolysates. This provides an alternative to the classical Strecker route that bypasses the need for preformed lactaldehyde. The mechanistic details are less specified than the Ritson-Sutherland route, and AMN accumulation remains an open question (Green et al. 2021, JACS). Clay surfaces may help concentrate AMN and aldehyde reactants.

reactions.name: Aminonitrile hydrolysis: threonine aminonitrile → threonine

conditions.other: Mild aqueous conditions; stepwise hydrolysis: aminonitrile → amide (via amidonitrile) → amino acid; can proceed slowly at neutral pH or more rapidly at mildly acidic/basic pH; produces racemic DL-threonine + DL-allothreonine mixture

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (-C≡N) of threonine aminonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first to an amide (-CONH₂) by addition of H₂O across the C≡N triple bond, then further hydrolysis of the amide to a carboxylic acid (-COOH) with loss of NH₃. The amino group at C2 and hydroxyl at C3 are retained. Product is racemic (allo-)threonine. This is the universal final step in all Strecker amino acid syntheses.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is the well-established final step of the Strecker synthesis. The Kyushu University meteoritic analysis explicitly frames hydrolysis of aminonitriles as producing α-amino acids, and identifies this as the terminal step for threonine formation from lactaldehyde-derived Strecker intermediates. Ritson & Sutherland (2013) and Green et al. (2021, JACS) assume this hydrolysis as the completion of their cyanosulfidic route to amino acids. The hydrolysis is spontaneous in aqueous solution over geochemical timescales.

pathways.name: Full cyanosulfidic photoredox from hydrothermal CO₂
pathways.description: Complete pathway from hydrothermal CO₂ fixation through surface cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry to threonine. CO₂ is reduced to formate then formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents, transported to surface pools where it combines with volcanic HCN to form glycolonitrile, which undergoes UV photoredox to glycolaldehyde/acetaldehyde, then cyanohydrin formation to lactonitrile, photoredox reduction to lactaldehyde, Strecker assembly to threonine aminonitrile, and hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Volcanic discharge HCN/HCHO → cyanosulfidic photoredox → Strecker threonine
pathways.description: Volcanic spark discharge provides HCN and formaldehyde, which combine to form glycolonitrile. UV cyanosulfidic photoredox reduces glycolonitrile to glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde. Acetaldehyde is converted to lactonitrile (cyanohydrin), then photoredox-reduced to lactaldehyde, and Strecker chemistry yields threonine aminonitrile → threonine.

pathways.name: Volcanic discharge direct acetaldehyde → photoredox lactaldehyde → Strecker threonine
pathways.description: Shortest photoredox pathway: volcanic discharge directly provides acetaldehyde and HCN. Acetaldehyde forms lactonitrile cyanohydrin with HCN, photoredox reduction yields lactaldehyde, Strecker assembly with HCN and NH₃ (both from discharge) gives threonine aminonitrile → threonine. Only 5 reaction steps.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal FTT acetaldehyde + volcanic HCN → photoredox → surface Strecker
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde from hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis (rxn_004) meets volcanic discharge-derived HCN (rxn_001) in surface pools. Cyanohydrin formation yields lactonitrile, UV photoredox produces lactaldehyde, and surface Strecker chemistry assembles threonine aminonitrile. Cross-environmental: Hydrothermal + Surface → Biochemical.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal FTT acetaldehyde → lactaldehyde → hydrothermal Strecker
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde (FTT) and volcanic HCN combine in surface pools to form lactonitrile, photoredox reduction yields lactaldehyde, and lactaldehyde undergoes Strecker chemistry in a hydrothermal vent-flank setting with mineral buffering. CAUTION: threonine not explicitly confirmed in hydrothermal Strecker experiments; hydroxy amino acid degradation at high T is a concern.

pathways.name: AMN route with volcanic discharge acetaldehyde
pathways.description: Volcanic discharge produces HCN, NH₃, and acetaldehyde. HCN trimerizes to aminomalononitrile (AMN) on clay surfaces. AMN condenses with discharge-derived acetaldehyde to form threonine aminonitrile directly, bypassing the need for lactaldehyde. Hydrolysis yields threonine. Most compact AMN route.

pathways.name: AMN route with hydrothermal FTT acetaldehyde
pathways.description: HCN from volcanic discharge trimerizes to AMN (surface), while acetaldehyde is independently sourced from hydrothermal FTT synthesis. AMN and FTT-acetaldehyde condense to form threonine aminonitrile. Cross-environmental: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde + Surface AMN → Biochemical hydrolysis.

pathways.name: AMN route with cyanosulfidic photoredox-derived acetaldehyde
pathways.description: Longest AMN pathway: hydrothermal CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde → glycolonitrile (with volcanic HCN) → photoredox yields acetaldehyde as co-product. Separately, HCN trimerizes to AMN. AMN condenses with photoredox-derived acetaldehyde to form threonine aminonitrile → threonine. Spans all three environments.

pathways.name: Volcanic discharge → lactaldehyde → hydrothermal Strecker threonine
pathways.description: Volcanic discharge provides HCN, NH₃, and acetaldehyde. Surface cyanohydrin + photoredox chemistry produces lactaldehyde. Lactaldehyde and HCN/NH₃ then undergo Strecker synthesis in a hydrothermal vent-flank mineral-buffered setting. This tests the boundary between surface and hydrothermal Strecker chemistry.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal origin → cyanosulfidic chain → hydrothermal Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: Maximum cross-environmental pathway: CO₂ reduced to formate then formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents, FTT also produces acetaldehyde. Formaldehyde + volcanic HCN → glycolonitrile in surface pools. Photoredox yields more acetaldehyde. Cyanohydrin + photoredox yields lactaldehyde. Lactaldehyde returns to hydrothermal environment for mineral-buffered Strecker synthesis. Threonine aminonitrile hydrolysis in mild pool. Traverses Hydrothermal → Surface → Hydrothermal → Biochemical.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longest pathway; formate-to-formaldehyde reduction (rxn_003) is thermodynamically difficult and poorly documented experimentally. The glycolonitrile-to-acetaldehyde coproduction in rxn_006 is also chemically unclear (requires both reduction and loss of OH). Cross-environmental flow from hydrothermal CO₂ fixation through surface UV chemistry is conceptually strong, but the early hydrothermal steps are the weakest links.
suggestions: Replace rxn_003 with a more established route to formaldehyde (e.g., UV photolysis of methanol or direct volcanic delivery)
Clarify the mechanism by which glycolonitrile produces acetaldehyde; consider separating glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde formation into distinct reaction nodes

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the core Ritson–Sutherland cyanosulfidic photoredox pathway, well-supported by literature. Spark discharge provides HCN and formaldehyde, cyanohydrin formation leads to glycolonitrile, UV cyanosulfidic photoreduction yields glycolaldehyde/acetaldehyde, second-stage photoreduction gives lactaldehyde, and Strecker assembly completes to threonine. The 42% yield for glycolaldehyde and 19% for lactaldehyde are experimentally documented. Minor concern: rxn_006 producing acetaldehyde as a co-product from glycolonitrile photoreduction should specify more precisely how the C–O bond is lost. Missing explicit CuCN catalyst specification for rxn_006 and rxn_008.
suggestions: Specify CuCN as mineral photoredox catalyst in rxn_006 and rxn_008
Consider whether acetaldehyde coproduction in rxn_006 is a separate mechanistic event or comes from glycolaldehyde rearrangement

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Elegant short pathway using acetaldehyde produced directly by spark discharge, followed by cyanohydrin formation (lactonitrile), UV photoreduction to lactaldehyde, Strecker assembly, and hydrolysis. All individual steps are well-precedented. The discharge production of acetaldehyde alongside HCN and NH₃ is experimentally confirmed (Parker et al. 2011, Ring et al. 1972). This is one of the most plausible and concise routes in the network.
suggestions: Specify CuCN catalyst and H₂S reductant more explicitly in rxn_008

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cross-environmental pathway combining hydrothermal FTT acetaldehyde with volcanic HCN. The FTT synthesis of acetaldehyde (rxn_004) is the weakest link—while FTT synthesis of hydrocarbons and simple oxygenates is documented on Fe/Ni minerals, specific yields of acetaldehyde are poorly quantified. The remainder of the pathway (cyanohydrin → photoreduction → Strecker) is solid.
suggestions: Provide more specific mineral catalyst for FTT (e.g., magnetite, awaruite)
Consider whether acetaldehyde survives transport from hydrothermal to surface environment

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway relies heavily on hydrothermal conditions for both acetaldehyde production (FTT) and Strecker assembly. Literature explicitly notes that threonine is NOT among the amino acids reported from hydrothermal Strecker experiments (Andersson & Holm 2000), and hydroxy amino acids undergo dehydration at elevated temperatures. The hydrothermal Strecker step (rxn_010) is the major vulnerability. Additionally, there is an environmental coherence issue: rxn_008 (UV photoreduction to lactaldehyde) is a Surface reaction sandwiched between hydrothermal steps.
suggestions: Consider replacing hydrothermal Strecker (rxn_010) with surface Strecker (rxn_009) given hydroxy amino acid instability at high T
Address the environmental transition: lactaldehyde made by UV photoreduction at surface would need to be transported back to hydrothermal zone for rxn_010

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short AMN-based route grounded in Thanassi (1975). Spark discharge provides both HCN and acetaldehyde; HCN trimerizes to AMN; AMN condenses with acetaldehyde to give threonine aminonitrile; hydrolysis yields threonine. Chemically plausible and experimentally supported, though AMN accumulation at useful concentrations (avoiding runaway HCN polymerization) remains a recognized challenge in the literature. The pathway elegantly bypasses the lactaldehyde bottleneck.
suggestions: Specify conditions (pH, temperature) under which HCN trimerization favors AMN over higher polymers

pathway_evaluations.feedback: AMN route using hydrothermal FTT acetaldehyde. Combines hydrothermal CO₂ fixation with surface AMN chemistry. Good environmental flow but limited by the speculative FTT acetaldehyde step. Otherwise follows the same Thanassi mechanism as P6.
suggestions: Strengthen the FTT acetaldehyde evidence or provide alternative hydrothermal acetaldehyde source

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Long route combining cyanosulfidic acetaldehyde generation with AMN condensation. Somewhat redundant: if you have the cyanosulfidic apparatus running, the direct lactaldehyde → Strecker route (P2/P3) is more efficient. The AMN branch adds complexity without clear benefit when acetaldehyde is already being routed through the cyanosulfidic channel. Chemical feasibility is adequate but pathway coherence suffers from unnecessary length.
suggestions: This pathway could be simplified; it represents a hybrid that is less efficient than either the pure cyanosulfidic or pure AMN route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Similar to P5 but starts from volcanic discharge. The hydrothermal Strecker step (rxn_010) remains problematic given the lack of experimental evidence for threonine from hydrothermal Strecker and the known instability of hydroxy amino acids at elevated temperatures. The environmental retrograde (surface UV photoreduction → hydrothermal Strecker) is also geologically awkward.
suggestions: Use surface Strecker (rxn_009) instead of hydrothermal (rxn_010) to avoid thermal degradation of threonine

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longest pathway combining the weakest hydrothermal steps (formate→formaldehyde, FTT acetaldehyde) with the problematic hydrothermal Strecker. While it demonstrates maximal cross-environmental connectivity, each weak link compounds. The formate-to-formaldehyde reduction and hydrothermal Strecker for a hydroxy amino acid are both poorly supported.
suggestions: Replace rxn_003 with a better-supported formaldehyde source

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed synthesis network for threonine that closely mirrors the literature landscape. The network correctly identifies lactaldehyde as the key carbonyl hub for threonine biosynthesis and implements three experimentally grounded routes: (1) the Ritson–Sutherland cyanosulfidic photoredox chain, (2) spark-discharge precursor generation, and (3) the Thanassi AMN condensation. Hub molecules (HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, threonine aminonitrile) are well-chosen and provide genuine convergence. Literature alignment is excellent—the network references all major experimental studies and reflects known yields and conditions. Key strengths: (i) multiple convergent routes to the target, (ii) realistic environmental transitions with hydrothermal CO₂ fixation feeding into surface UV chemistry, (iii) correct identification of the lactaldehyde bottleneck. Key weaknesses: (i) the formate-to-formaldehyde hydrothermal reduction (rxn_003) is thermodynamically uphill and poorly documented; (ii) FTT synthesis of acetaldehyde specifically (rxn_004) is speculative; (iii) hydrothermal Strecker synthesis for threonine (rxn_010) lacks direct experimental support and conflicts with known hydroxy amino acid thermal instability; (iv) surface photoredox reactions (rxn_006, rxn_008) should explicitly specify CuCN as the mineral photoredox catalyst per Ritson & Sutherland; (v) rxn_006 producing acetaldehyde as a co-product of glycolonitrile photoreduction needs mechanistic clarification. Despite these issues, four pathways (P2, P3, P4, P6) achieve approval-level confidence, and the overall network architecture is sound and well-grounded in the experimental prebiotic chemistry literature.
